comments:
Summary:
This paper presents a two-timescale learning architecture for mobile robot navigation that uses a fast, simulation-based Genetic Algorithm (LTL) to produce diverse behaviour sets which seed an idiotypic Artificial Immune System (STL) running on a physical e-puck robot (and in simulation). The authors compare seeded vs unseeded initialisation, idiotypic vs non-idiotypic STL, and include a hand-designed baseline. They report that (i) multiple autonomous GA populations yield greater behavioural diversity without slowing convergence, (ii) seeded idiotypic AIS achieves the best performance (including zero failure rate in trials), and (iii) evolved behaviours transfer well from simulation to the real world across structurally different environments.

Strengths:
- Well-motivated combination of evolutionary (LTL) seeding and lifetime/adaptive (STL) idiotypic selection; biologically inspired and plausible.
- Thorough experimental campaign: both simulated and real-robot trials, multiple environment variants, and several baselines (unseeded, non-idiotypic, hand-designed).
- Quantitative metrics (task time, collisions, solution quality) and a clear set of hypotheses tested; use of multiple GA population modes and diversity measures is useful.
- Results indicate practical training times and convincing improvements from the combined approach; real-world transfer is demonstrated.

Weaknesses and points for improvement:
- Experimental scope could be broadened: tasks/environments are still relatively structured and constrained (target-finding in small arenas). It is unclear how the approach scales to more complex, unstructured, or larger-scale tasks.
- Limited baselines: only one hand-designed controller is used. Comparisons to other adaptive controllers (e.g. purely RL-based, behavior-based mediation without idiotypic networks, or other AIS variants) would strengthen claims.
- Some algorithmic choices and parameter settings (e.g. k1,k2, b, scaling constants, reward magnitudes, mutation rate) appear to be tuned via pre-trials; more discussion of sensitivity and robustness to these choices would be helpful.
- Statistical reporting: the paper uses t-tests at the 99% level, but more complete presentation of statistical measures (e.g. confidence intervals, effect sizes) would aid interpretation. The number of real-world trials is smaller (20) than simulated (30); that is understandable but should be explicitly discussed w.r.t. statistical power.
- A small practical concern: replacement is disabled for seeded systems—while experiments suggest this is fine here, it limits long-term adaptability in changing environments. Some ablation on longer-term adaptation would be valuable.
- Clarity: the paper is dense in places and would benefit from a clearer schematic of the full pipeline (LTL→storage→STL) and a concise summary of algorithmic parameters and pseudocode.

Recommendations for authors:
- Provide sensitivity analysis for key parameters and report whether conclusions hold across those variations.
- Broaden baselines where possible (other adaptive controllers) and consider more varied, less structured environments.
- Include more detailed statistical reporting and discussion of experimental power, especially for the real-robot trials.
- Discuss limitations concerning scalability and long-term adaptability when the environment changes post-seeding.

Overall assessment:
A solid and well-executed study that provides useful empirical evidence for the benefits of seeding an idiotypic AIS with evolved behaviours and demonstrates successful sim-to-real transfer. Recommended for publication after modest revisions/clarifications as noted above.
